title: Configure encryption for Azure Elastic SAN volumes
display_title: Configure CMK-based encryption for Elastic SAN volumes
description: Learn how to configure Azure Elastic SAN encryption with customer-managed keys (CMK) for volumes provisioned via Azure Container Storage by using Azure CLI.
summary: All data written to an Elastic SAN volume is encrypted at rest with a data encryption key (DEK). Azure usesenvelope encryptionto encrypt the DEK with a key encryption key (KEK). By default, Azure uses a platform-managed KEK. You can use a customer-managed key (CMK) stored in Azure Key Vault. This article shows how to configure encryption for an Elastic SAN volume group by using a CMK in Azure Key Vault.
classification_reason: Describes configuring encryption with customer-managed keys in Key Vault; likely includes specific CLI parameters, Key Vault resource IDs, and role/permission requirements.

title: Resize persistent volumes in Azure Container Storage
display_title: Resize Azure Container Storage volumes within capacity limits
description: Resize persistent volumes in Azure Container Storage without downtime. Scale up by expanding volumes backed by Elastic SAN and local NVMe.
summary: You can expand persistent volumes in Azure Container Storage without downtime. Shrinking persistent volumes isn't supported. You can't increase a volume beyond the maximum capacity available in your Elastic SAN or the local NVMe storage available on your nodes. If you need more capacity, firstincrease your Elastic SAN capacityorincrease your ephemeral disk (local NVMe) capacityby adding more nodes to your Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster. Then expand the volume size.
classification_reason: Discusses constraints like not shrinking volumes and maximum capacity tied to Elastic SAN or local NVMe; likely includes explicit size limits or rules that function as quotas.
